# Continuous Improvement Analysis for COMP 110!

## Finding ways to improve student experience in COMP 110!

<!-- This is a comment. Below, you'll see code for inserting an image. To make this image appear, update <custom-path>. To add an image, save it inside the imgs folder of this repository. -->
<img src="/ablauren.ex09.website/static/imgs/logo.png" alt="Image of Comp110 rainbow logo. "  width="500"/>


Hypothesis: Increasing the pace at the beginning of the course so the more difficult material can be slowed down will help reduce stress for students and give them more time to master concepts.

If students found the pace to be too fast at the time the survey was taken, it may have been beneficial to speed up some of the earlier concepts in order to spend more time on the later concepts.

Does the fast pace of this course increase the difficulty for students?

Yes!

I first analyzed an overall distribution of students perception on course pace on the survey conducted towards the end of the semester. 

<img src="/ablauren.ex09.website/static/imgs/Seaborn.plot.1.png" alt="Screenshot" width="400"/>

The average rating on a scale of 1 (slow paced) to 7 (fast paced) wasa 4.6.

Next ... 
Did the fast pace of the course increase perceieved difficulty? 



There was a positive correlation between perceievd course pace and perceieved, difficultly, however this does not prove a cause/effect relationship/ 

<img src="/static/imgs/Seaborn.plot.2.png" width = "400"/>

People who felt the course was too fast paced also rated the course higher on difficulty. 

Next... How did people feel about their undestanding of course concepts relative to the pace of the course?

<img src= "/static/imgs/Seaborn.plot.3.png" width = "400"/>

People who felt the course was going to fast had less understanding of course material based on thier responses to the survey. 

A positive note! People who feel like the course is too faced paced take advantage of office hours for assignments!

<img src = "/static/imgs/Seaborn.plot.4.png" width = "400"/>

## Conclusion

The analysis overall supported my idea that the fast paced nature of the course may have made this course more difficult for students. The average rating on a scale of 1(slow paced) to 7 (fast paced) wasa 4.6. I found that students who percieved the course to be more fast paced had lower levels of understanding and rated the course higher on the difficulty scale, suggesting that the pace of the course is an important factor in how challenging a course is for someone. A more positive finding, however, was that those who percieved the course as being fast paced also tended to utilize additional help resources, such as office hours. I woulc recommend spending more time on concepts towards the end of the course, and perhaps doing in class problems that are more similar to the exercise questions. 

A negative impact could be the pacing of asisignments or additional work that may need to be done by the instructional team to adjust the course.  I interpreted the question as asking about the pace as which new content was taught in the course, but the pacing of homework assignments and quizzes must also be considered. If the easier concepts were to be taught quicker while spending more time on the later and more difficult concepts, this may lead to assignments being spaced closer together at the beginning of the course or further apart at the end of this course. I don't think this is necessarily a negative effect, as students tend to have more work later in the semester, so geting more of the assignments out of the way earlier may be beneficial. Another limitation is that this data does not analyze perceptions of the course pace and difficulty over time. The survey was conducted later in the course, which is why I used the data to claim that the latter material may be slightly too fast paced. However, students who percieve the course as fast paced may have also struggled with concepts earlier in the course, and speeding up the foundational lectures may actual worsen thier ability to understand later material. Conducting this survey at the beginning and end of the course may help to see how student perspectives change throughout the course.
